---
title: "Manuscript Preparation & Peer Review"
---

::: {.topic-badges}
[Advanced]{.level-badge .level-advanced} · **Communication & Dissemination** track · ~1 hour, self-paced
:::

[← Back to the Communication & Dissemination track](communication.qmd){.track-back}

*Use AI to prepare manuscripts and assist review — within strict confidentiality and disclosure rules.*

## What you'll be able to do

- Draft and tighten manuscript sections, abstracts, and reviewer responses
- Apply reporting checklists (STROBE/CONSORT) with AI help
- Respect confidentiality and journal AI policies in peer review

## Overview

AI can draft a methods paragraph, tighten a results narrative, structure a cover letter, and organize point-by-point reviewer responses. The analysis, interpretation, and authorship remain yours, and AI use is typically disclosed.

Peer review has a hard rule: a manuscript you're reviewing is **confidential**. Do not paste an unpublished manuscript into an AI tool, and follow each journal's policy on AI in review — many prohibit it outright.

## Practice activities

```{=html}
<details class="activity"><summary>Activity 1 · Advanced — Tighten a section against a reporting checklist</summary>
```

**Time** ~18 min · **Tools** ChatGPT Edu

**Goal.** Use a reporting guideline to strengthen your own methods section.

**Setup.** Your own draft methods section (non-sensitive) and the relevant checklist (e.g., STROBE for observational studies).

**Steps.**

1. Find the gaps:

   > Here is my methods section and the STROBE checklist. Which checklist items are missing, unclear, or under-reported? Be specific. [methods] [checklist]

2. Revise to address them; confirm each change is true to what you actually did.

3. Improve reproducibility:

   > Suggest where I should add detail to make this reproducible, without padding.

**Expected result.** A checklist-aligned methods section.

**Check your work.** Every added detail must match your real methods — don’t let AI invent procedures you didn’t use.

**Common pitfalls.** AI “improves” by adding things you didn’t do. You own accuracy. Use an approved tool for unpublished work.

**Stretch (optional).** Generate a STROBE/CONSORT compliance table to submit with the paper.

```{=html}
</details>
```

```{=html}
<details class="activity"><summary>Activity 2 · Advanced — Structure a response to reviewers</summary>
```

**Time** ~15 min · **Tools** ChatGPT Edu

**Goal.** Organize a clear, point-by-point reviewer response — while respecting confidentiality.

**Setup.** Your own reviewer comments on your paper and your planned replies. **Never paste a manuscript you are reviewing for a journal.**

**Steps.**

1. Organize the letter:

   > Organize these reviewer comments and my planned responses into a professional, point-by-point response letter grouped by reviewer, with polite framing. [comments + your replies]

2. Verify every “we have revised…” statement is actually done in the manuscript.

3. Soften where needed:

   > Flag any response that sounds defensive and suggest a more constructive phrasing.

**Expected result.** A clean response-to-reviewers letter.

**Check your work.** Confirm each claim of a change matches the revised manuscript.

**Common pitfalls.** Hard rule: never put a manuscript you’re peer-reviewing into an AI tool — it’s confidential and many journals prohibit it. Disclose AI use in your own writing.

**Stretch (optional).** Draft a short cover letter to the editor summarizing the major changes.

```{=html}
</details>
```


## Check your readiness

Answer these, then check — your score suggests whether to dive in or skim the readings first.

<div class="readiness-quiz">
<script type="application/json" class="quiz-data">
{"questions": [{"q": "A manuscript you're peer-reviewing is…", "options": ["Confidential — don't put it in an AI tool", "Fine to paste anywhere", "Public", "Yours to share"], "answer": 0, "explain": "Review confidentiality is a firm rule; many journals prohibit AI in review."}, {"q": "In your own manuscript, AI use is typically…", "options": ["Disclosed per journal/ICMJE guidance", "Hidden", "Required", "Irrelevant"], "answer": 0, "explain": "Disclose AI assistance; AI can't be an author."}, {"q": "What does AI not do for you here?", "options": ["Own the analysis, interpretation, and authorship", "Draft text", "Tighten language", "Format a letter"], "answer": 0, "explain": "Substance and authorship stay with you."}]}
</script>
</div>

## Recommended readings

From the AI readings library (ask the facilitator for access):

- **Peer Review Is Dead; Long Live Peer Review!** — how AI is reshaping the review system.
- **AI-Slop, GrantaGate and Bad Writing** — cautionary view of AI-generated academic text.

## Useful resources

- [ICMJE Recommendations](https://www.icmje.org) — authorship and AI-disclosure guidance.
- [EQUATOR Network](https://www.equator-network.org) — reporting guidelines (STROBE, CONSORT, etc.).
- [ChatGPT Edu (Drexel AI Tools)](https://drexel.edu/provost/ai/tools) — the approved tool, with data rules.

::: {.callout-warning}
## Data rules
**Never** paste a manuscript you're reviewing into any AI tool; use approved tools for your own drafts and disclose AI use. See [Tools & Access](../tools.qmd) and Drexel's [AI Tools page](https://drexel.edu/provost/ai/tools).
:::
